chunk_summary: Guidelines for selecting age-appropriate growth references and indices to improve diagnostic accuracy in stunting, undernutrition, and growth assessment, with emphasis on WHO standards and national alternatives.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of selecting age-appropriate growth references and indices for accurate growth assessment.
Learn to apply WHO child growth standards for ages 0–59 months and WHO growth references for ages 5–19 years.
Recognize the significance of corrected age for preterm infants and mid-parental height in evaluating short stature.
Identify key growth patterns and body proportions to refine differential diagnoses and reduce unnecessary testing.
key_facts: For ages 0–59 months, use WHO child growth standards: LAZ/HAZ for stunting, weight-for-height for wasting, and weight-for-age for underweight.
For ages 5–19 years, use WHO growth references or national alternatives like IAP charts in India.
Corrected age should be used for preterm infants to avoid misinterpreting expected catch-up growth.
Mid-parental height helps assess if a child is below genetic potential, which is concerning even if above -2 SD.
Growth velocity and pubertal tempo are critical for interpreting growth in children aged 5–19 years.
Documenting growth patterns (tracking vs. crossing centiles) and body proportions aids in identifying skeletal dysplasia.
reference_quote: For ages 5–19 years, interpret height-for-age and BMI-for-age using the WHO growth reference for 5–19 years, or nationally recommended alternatives such as the IAP charts commonly used in India.
document_key_topics: WHO child growth standards and national alternatives
Short stature evaluation and differential diagnosis
